{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804年]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.

The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}}

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}}

{{神社 |名称= 滝祭神 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image= [[File:Takimatsuri-no-kami 01.jpg|270px]] |所在地= [[三重県]][[伊勢市]]宇治館町1 内宮宮域内 |緯度度 = 34 | 緯度分 = 27 | 緯度秒 = 19.8 |経度度 = 136 |経度分 = 43 | 経度秒 = 18.8 |地図= Japan Mie |祭神= 瀧祭大神 |社格= {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}}所管社 |創建= [[延暦]]23年（[[804]]）以前 |本殿= なし |別名= おとりつぎさん |札所等= |例祭= |神事= 由貴大御饌、[[神嘗祭]] }}
== Overview ==
'''Takimatsuri-no-Kami''' (滝祭神) is a shrine under the jurisdiction of {{ill|Kōtai Jingū|ja|皇大神宮|1=Kōtai Jingū|2=ja|3=皇大神宮|4=zh|5=皇大神宮}} (Naikū) of {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within the precincts of Naikū, near the {{ill|Mitarashi|ja|御手洗場}}<ref name="ic">Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.112</ref>.
Despite being a shrine without a {{ill|shaden (shrine building)|ja|社殿}}, it receives rituals equivalent to those of a {{ill|Betsugū (auxiliary shrine)|ja|別宮}}, making it a special shrine within the Naikū precincts<ref name="ic"/>.
== Overview ==
Situated in {{ill|Ujikanda-chō|ja|宇治館町}}, {{ill|Ise City|ja|伊勢市}}, Mie Prefecture, within the grounds of Naikū, Takimatsuri-no-Kami is ranked first among the 30 shrines under Naikū's jurisdiction. As the enshrined deity is the {{ill|water deity|ja|水神}} of the {{ill|Isuzu River (Mie)|ja|五十鈴川}}, which symbolizes the location of Naikū, rituals similar to those of auxiliary shrines are conducted<ref name="ic"/>. The {{ill|Ujiyamada City History|ja|宇治山田市史}} notes:
{{Cquote|Since ancient times, Takimatsuri-no-Kami has been honored with offerings akin to those presented at auxiliary shrines, thus ranked particularly between the auxiliary shrines and the associate shrines.}}
Locals affectionately refer to it as "Otoritsugi-san" (おとりつぎさん)<ref name="ic"/>. It is believed that praying to Takimatsuri-no-Kami before visiting the main shrine will help convey one’s wishes to {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk">Gakken Publishing (2013): p.55</ref>.
There is no shrine building; instead, a sacred stone is enshrined within the {{ill|Itagaki (sacred fence)|ja|板垣}}<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>. The reason for the absence of a shrine building remains unknown even to those involved with Ise Jingu. The mystery deepens with the offering of purification rituals, and even the "Daijingū Gishiki-kai" (1775) only states, "It has profound significance"<ref>Hozumi (2013): pp.160–161</ref>. While most shrines under Ise Jingu do not have offering boxes<ref>Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p.26</ref>, Takimatsuri-no-Kami does have one.
== Enshrined Deity ==
The enshrined deity is '''Takimatsuri-no-Ōkami''' (瀧祭大神)<ref name="ic"/>. This deity represents the {{ill|source of the Isuzu River|ja|五十鈴川の水源}}<ref name="ic"/> and is worshipped for the purpose of {{ill|flood control|ja|治水}}, as it sits at the confluence of the Isuzu and Shimaji Rivers<ref name="ic"/><ref name="gk"/>.
== History ==
The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Enryaku Gishikichō|ja|延暦儀式帳}} as "Takimatsuri Shrine" and "Takimatsuri no Chi" (滝祭の地)<ref name="gs">Sakurai (1970): p.165</ref>. However, it is not listed in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} or the {{ill|Rikkokushi|ja|六国史}}, making it an {{ill|shikigai-sha (non-listed shrine)|ja|式外社}}<ref name="gs"/>.
According to the {{ill|Kenkyū Annual Rituals|ja|建久年中行事}}, water from the Isuzu River was drawn at "Takimatsuri no Se" and delivered to the {{ill|Saikū|ja|斎宮}} in bamboo tubes during special festivals<ref name="gs3">Sakurai (1970): p.120</ref>. While the exact location of "Takimatsuri no Se" is unknown, it is estimated to be somewhere between the first {{ill|torii|ja|鳥居}} and Takimatsuri-no-Kami<ref name="gs3"/>.
The practice of {{ill|offering sacred cloth (hōbei)|ja|奉幣}} began during the Meiji period<ref>Sakurai (1970): pp.165–166</ref>.
== Rituals ==
The '''Yuki-no-Ōmike''' (由貴大御饌) ceremony is performed by opening the sacred gate within the Itagaki fence<ref name="gs"/>. The sacred rice offerings are cultivated in the sacred rice fields of Ise Jingu and stored in the {{ill|Mishine-no-Mikura|ja|御稲御倉}}, the same as those dedicated to Naikū and {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮}}<ref>Sakurai (1970): p.156</ref>.
During the {{ill|Kannamesai|ja|神嘗祭}}, a great banquet ({{ill|Naorai|ja|直会}}) is held following the rituals, followed by Takimatsuri ceremonies<ref name="gs2">Sakurai (1970): p.166</ref>. After the Yuki-no-Ōmike and Takimatsuri ceremonies, another Naorai is conducted<ref name="gs2"/>. The post-Takimatsuri Naorai was an important ritual, as only two individuals, the {{ill|negi (Shinto priest)|ja|禰宜}} and Uji Ōuchi-no-hito, were permitted to partake in newly harvested rice and sake<ref name="gs2"/>.
== Access ==
; To {{ill|Ujibashi Bridge|ja|宇治橋}} * About 15 minutes by {{ill|Mie Kotsu|ja|三重交通}} bus from the south exit (JR side) of {{ill|Ise Station|ja|伊勢市駅}} on the {{ill|JR Sangū Line|ja|参宮線}} or from {{ill|Ujiyamada Station|ja|宇治山田駅}} on the Kintetsu Yamada and Toba Lines, alighting at the final stop "Naikū-mae"<ref name="mkr">Mie Prefecture Tourism Federation, "[http://www.kankomie.or.jp/spot/detail_2937.html Ise Jingu (Naikū) Tourist Info - Visit Mie]" (Accessed October 12, 2013)</ref>. Also accessible via bus from {{ill|Isuzugawa Station|ja|五十鈴川駅}} on the Kintetsu Toba Line. * About 5 minutes south via {{ill|Mie Prefectural Route 32|ja|三重県道32号伊勢磯部線}} (Mikimoto Road) from {{ill|Ise-nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the {{ill|Ise Expressway|ja|伊勢自動車道}}, or from {{ill|Ise Interchange|ja|伊勢インターチェンジ}} via {{ill|National Route 23 (Japan)|ja|国道23号}}. Parking available nearby<ref name="mkr"/>.
; From Ujibashi Bridge * Cross Ujibashi, proceed through the sacred grounds (Shinen), pass over the {{ill|Hiyoke Bridge|ja|火除橋}}, and through the first torii gate of Naikū to reach the Mitarashi area<ref name="ic"/>. Takimatsuri-no-Kami is located just south of the Mitarashi, nestled within a grove<ref name="gs"/>.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|List of 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧}}
